text: Once David was settled in his palace and the LORD had given him rest from every enemy around him,
the king said to Nathan the prophet that he lived in a house of cedar while the ark of God stayed in a tent.
Nathan told the king to go ahead with whatever was on his mind, since the LORD was with him.
But that very night, a message from the LORD came to Nathan.
He was to tell David: are you the one who should build me a house to live in?
God had not lived in a house since bringing Israel out of Egypt, but had moved about in a tent the whole time.
In all his travels with Israel, had he ever asked any tribal leader why they hadn't built him a house of cedar?
Tell David instead: the LORD took him from tending sheep in the pasture and made him ruler over his people Israel.
God had been with him everywhere he went, cut down his enemies, and would make his name as great as any on earth.
God would establish a place for Israel and plant them there so they would settle down and stop being harassed as they had been before.
Ever since the days of the judges, and now, the LORD would give David rest from his enemies, and would build David himself a house.
When David's life was over and he was buried with his ancestors, God would raise up a son from his own body and make his kingdom secure.
That son would build a house for God's name, and God would make his royal throne last forever.
God would be a father to him and he would be a son to God; if he sinned, he would be corrected as any son would be.
But God's faithful love would never leave him the way it left Saul, whom God removed to make room for David.
David's house and kingdom would stand secure forever in God's sight, his throne established for all time.
Nathan told David everything, word for word, exactly as it had been shown to him.
Then King David went in, sat down before the LORD, and asked who he was and what his family was, that God had brought him this far.
He said this was still a small thing to God, who had also spoken about his family's distant future — remarkable treatment for any man.
David admitted there was nothing more he could say, since God already knew his servant fully.
For the sake of his own word and his own purposes, God had done this great thing and made it known to his servant.
David praised how great the LORD God is — there is no one like him, no god besides him, exactly as everyone had always heard.
He asked what other nation on earth was like Israel, whom God went and redeemed as his own people, making a name for himself with great and awesome deeds.
God had made Israel his own people forever, and had become their God.
David asked the LORD to keep the promise he had made about his servant's house forever, and to do exactly as he had said.
Then God's name would be praised forever, and people would say the LORD of hosts is God over Israel, with David's house standing secure before him.
Because the LORD of hosts, Israel's God, had revealed to David that he would build him a house, David found the courage to pray this prayer.
David affirmed that the Lord GOD is God, that his words are trustworthy, and that he had promised this good thing to his servant.
So David asked God to go ahead and bless his house so it would continue before him forever, since the Lord GOD himself had spoken it.
